title: The growth equation of cities
data_sources: US Census Bureau American Community Survey 2013-2017

data_scope.spatial_unit: metropolitan statistical areas / urban units / census metropolitan areas / local authorities

core_conclusion: City population growth is governed by rare, large interurban migratory shocks that follow a Lévy-stable (heavy-tailed) distribution rather than the Gaussian shocks assumed by Gibrat's law. A stochastic differential equation built on this insight reproduces the full city-size distribution across Canada, France, the UK, and the USA, demonstrates that Zipf's law does not hold universally due to finite-time effects, and correctly predicts the observed frequency of large rank changes in city hierarchies.
why_it_matters: Overturns Zipf's law as a universal principle by identifying finite-time effects; establishes rare Lévy-distributed migration events as the dominant driver of urban hierarchy; provides a unified framework that simultaneously explains static size distributions and dynamic rank volatility; redirects planning attention from average trends to tail-event resilience.
why_nature: Fundamental result overturning a century-old empirical regularity; rigorous cross-disciplinary synthesis of statistical physics and urban economics; empirically validated across four countries; broad societal relevance for urban planning and sustainability.
added_value_to_my_research: Lévy-SDE framework applied to mobile-phone/CDR migration proxies enables cross-country calibration for developing-country accessibility analysis
Spatially resolved growth equation (tract-level) identifies which sub-city zones face sudden demand shocks, directly feeding 2SFCA service-area forecasting
Lévy-driven demand modelling quantifies tail risk of telehealth service overload for digital-divide and health-access research
Physics-informed GNN learning city-specific Lévy exponents α and β as functions of urban features — extending my physics-informed graph learning agenda
Regime-stratified regrowth equation calibrated on global demographic divergence data creates macroscopic context for GeoAI health-accessibility prioritization
